steps.title: Understand the two-study chain
steps.content:
Sharma & Dayal: higher perceived environmental effectiveness $\Rightarrow$ higher purchase likelihood. The South Korea study: college students gave the furniture the highest environmental effectiveness rating of any demographic group. Putting these together means college students should have the highest purchase likelihood.
  • Choice A — 'college students likely prioritize other factors over a product's environmental sustainability when making purchasing decisions.': Contradicts Sharma & Dayal, which shows environmental perception drives purchase likelihood. ✗
  • Choice B — 'there is not a meaningful difference in the average likelihood of purchasing environmentally friendly products among the demographic groups included in the study.': Contradicts the data, which shows college students rated effectiveness higher than all other groups. ✗
  • Choice C — 'the new piece of furniture is less appealing to college students than other similar products on the market are.': Introduces a comparison to other products not addressed anywhere in the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — 'college students might be more likely than participants in the other demographic groups to purchase the piece of furniture.': Directly follows from combining both studies: highest effectiveness rating $\Rightarrow$ highest purchase likelihood. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.

steps.title: Confirm the logical link
steps.content:
Sharma & Dayal's finding is stated to be “broadly applicable,” so it applies here. College students gave the highest environmental effectiveness rating $\Rightarrow$ they are the most likely to purchase. Choice D states exactly this conclusion without overstating it (“might be more likely” hedges appropriately).

That matches choice D — D is correct.
